title: Quadratic Cost of Self-Attention
concept: Because self-attention computes relationships between all tokens, the compute cost scales quadratically with sequence length, making long context expensive.

exercises.prompt: How does the computational cost of self-attention change as the text sequence gets longer?

options.text: It stays constant regardless of length

exercises.explain: Self-attention requires comparing every word to every other word in the sequence. If the sequence is length N, this requires N multiplied by N operations, which is quadratic scaling.

cards.title: The Cost of Long Texts
cards.body: Have you ever wondered why AI chatbots sometimes struggle when you paste an entire book into the prompt box? It isn't just about reading speed; it is because of a hidden math problem inside the AI that makes processing long text incredibly expensive!

cards.title: What is Quadratic Cost?
cards.body: Self-attention has a 'quadratic cost.' This means that if you double the length of your input text, the computational effort and memory required don't just double—they increase by four times! This makes reading long books or documents very difficult and expensive for AI models.

cards.title: The Holiday Card Swap
cards.body: Imagine writing holiday cards. If you write one card to each of your 10 friends, you write 10 cards. But if every friend has to write a unique card to every single other friend in the group, that is 100 cards in total! As the group gets larger, the total number of cards to write explodes. This is exactly how self-attention works: every word has to 'write a card' to every other word.

cards.title: Comparing Small and Large Inputs
cards.body: Let's look at the numbers. Processing a short sentence of 10 words requires about 100 comparisons (10 x 10). But if you try to process a long document of 1,000 words, it requires a massive 1,000,000 comparisons (1,000 x 1,000)! This is why AI can get slow or run out of memory with long prompts.

cards.title: Myth: Fast Computers Solve Everything
cards.body: You might think that upgrading to a faster computer instantly solves the problem of long context. But because the work grows quadratically, even the fastest supercomputers quickly run out of memory when texts get extremely long. We need smarter algorithms, not just faster chips!

cards.title: Comparing Grid Sizes
cards.body: Notice how the grid of comparisons grows. A small text fits in a small grid, but a larger text creates a massive grid of comparisons.

right.text: Creates a large 50x50 grid (2,500 comparisons) which is much heavier.

left.text: Creates a small 5x5 grid (25 comparisons) which is fast and cheap.

exercises.prompt: Why does the cost of self-attention grow so fast as text gets longer?

options.text: It only reads the very first and last words of the text.
It compares every word in the text with every other word.
It translates the text into multiple foreign languages at once.
It deletes unnecessary words to save computer space.

exercises.explain: Self-attention compares every single word in a text to every other word in that same text, creating a grid of relationships that grows rapidly.

exercises.prompt: True or False: If you double the length of a text prompt, the computational cost of self-attention only doubles.
exercises.explain: The cost increases by four times (2 squared = 4) because self-attention cost scales quadratically with length.

exercises.prompt: Fill in the missing word to describe how self-attention's computational cost scales with sequence length.
exercises.sentence: As the sequence length grows, the compute cost of self-attention scales quadratically.
exercises.explain: Because every word compares itself to every other word, the total work scales quadratically (as the square of the length), not linearly.

exercises.prompt: Two of these statements about self-attention cost are true, but one is an AI-generated error. Which one is the false AI error?

options.text: Because of quadratic scaling, doubling the text length requires four times the compute power.
Self-attention is cheap because its computational cost scales linearly with the input length.
Comparing every token to every other token makes long-context inputs very expensive.

exercises.explain: The statement 'It scales linearly' is incorrect (AI-generated mistake) because self-attention scales quadratically. The other two statements correctly describe the challenges of quadratic scaling in AI models.

exercises.prompt: If you double the length of your input text (from 1,000 words to 2,000 words), what happens to the computational cost of the self-attention steps?

options.text: It increases by a constant factor of 2

exercises.explain: When sequence length doubles, the self-attention cost quadruples (2 squared = 4) because every token must compare itself to every other token, including the new ones. This quadratic scaling makes long texts very computationally expensive to process.
